narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands above the typical 3.21 average, along with an LSAT score of 36—close to the usual 36.70 mark—and plans to attend part-time while managing what sounds like a middle-income family background. Your Year 1 school decile ranking placed you in the bottom 10%, but it's your Year 3 decile of 1.00, also in the lowest 10% compared to the average of around the middle of the pack, that weighed heavily in the model's eyes. The admission system uses data from past students to predict bar exam success on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 51% chance of failure—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 academic decile, as it signaled to the model a potential struggle to build momentum in later schooling, unlike many peers who climb higher. Countering that, your LSAT score had a strong favorable pull, nudging the prediction toward passing since it's competitively close to what's typical for successful bar takers. Your undergraduate GPA provided another helpful boost in the same direction, exceeding the norm and showing prior college-level promise. While your part-time status and family income played roles, these top three—Year 3 deciles most critically—dominated the decision, reflecting patterns where weaker late-stage school performance often correlates with bar challenges for students in similar situations. Compared to typical admitted applicants, who often rank higher in later deciles and edge out on standardized tests, your profile showed enough risk that the model prioritized caution. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male with clear drive—many in your demographic thrive in law—but a data-driven flag on that Year 3 dip. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps aiming for full-time study if feasible, and reapply after strengthening those areas; schools value growth stories, and targeted prep could flip this prediction next time. You're close—51% is no landslide—and with focus, you can rewrite this chapter.
